records.title: Brazil 2022 — right dynamics, wrong winner
records.claim:
[Sealed Sep 19 2022] With the blessings of my family deity Ootukulangara #Bhagavathy and Sri Maha #Kali Maha #Lakshmi Maha #Saraswathi, here is an #astrology based analysis related to the Presidential #elections in #Brazil.

The honorable #President Jair #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro is likely to defeat the honorable President #Lula Da Silva @LulaOficial with just over 50% of the #runoff vote. This will be a stunning reversal on Oct 30 after his first round loss to President Lula on Oct 2.

#President #Lula Da Silva @LulaOficial will win the first round on Oct 2 with President #Bolsonaro coming in second. President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro might even trail uncomfortably in the initial counting before closing the gap to take second place.

It appears that #oil prices and related #inflation pressures are on the minds of the people as they make their decision for the future of #Brazil. The #pandemic and related actions have little impact on the outcome.

The #election is likely to be marred by #political #violence. As such, the general #public is advised to keep their political affiliations private and not to discuss or share them publicly. Telling people what they want to hear is a good #strategy to be safe (even in general).

#President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro will find that despite his best efforts, he will be unable to prevent President #Lula Da Silva @LulaOficial from #winning the first round.

#President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro apparently senses this loss and has tried to keep his hopes up with faith in God. But after knowing the real ground situation, there seems to be an air of disappointment for him at this time.

However, the surprising #news is that #President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro might very well have the #last #laugh in the #runoff election and #Brazil will celebrate when he wins.

#President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro will find that as he opens up and speaks more freely and strongly on key #conservative policies, there will be a remarkable collapse in the gap between him and President #Lula. This is key to change his losing hand into a winning one.

#President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro will also benefit by providing quick and impactful relief (even if temporary) for rising #energy prices and announcing #policy positions that will attract the #vote from #women.

#President #Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro will do well to remember that losing the first round means nothing if he can pick himself up and win the #runoff (the real battle). The hope for his win remains if he truly believes.

This appears to be a test of faith from God that is dear to him and his evangelical base. #President Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro is likely to keep the faith and effect a remarkable turnaround in the runoff.

Post Oct 2, President Bolsonaro @jairbolsonaro is likely to continue to close the gap to President Lula Da Silva @LulaOficial with them competing head-to-head by Oct 15.

The #media will, as expected, write him-off before and after the Oct 2 results. Those that do are in for a surprise on Oct 30. This will likely be a notable comeback in the recent history of #elections in #Brazil.

I hope I've been of service to you. My best wishes to the people of #Brazil and the world all over. May #God bless us all with #peace, #prosperity and #freedom.

[Follow-up, Nov 8 2022] With the blessings of my family deity Oothukulangara Bhagavathy and Sri Lalitha Tripurāsundari, here's an astrological analysis of the near-term future for Brazil.

It appears that the honorable President Lula Da Silva is all set to be sworn in as the next President of Brazil. But he will likely run into strong headwinds almost immediately and as early as mid-February 2023. The situation is likely get progressively worse for the following 13 months.

February 2023 to March 2024 will also be a difficult time for the people of Brazil. There are strong indications suggesting the possibility of vindictive, vengeful, authoritarian and forceful actions by the leadership. Expect the strongest push you've ever seen for the V. This is the time period when the presidency of the honorable Lula Da Silva is likely to be at its weakest given public unrest.

It would be advisable for the honorable President Jair Bolsonaro to leave Brazil at the end of this year for his own personal safety. Otherwise, the risk of severe persecution and confinement under various charges of dereliction is a very real possibility. This might even result in him seeking refuge outside of Brazil for an extended period of time.

Until Feb 2024, the environment is likely to be characterized by financial irregularities, questionable ethics, controversies and socio-political crisis. There is clearly an 'invisible hand' at play behind these negative events which seeks to fulfill an ulterior motive.

Between June 2023 and July 2024, there will likely be increased public unrest. There will be events of agricultural, industrial and labor unrest which might bring the government functioning to a grinding halt. But this will eventually result in renaissance and reforms. This will however slow down Brazil's economy in the near term.

With regard to the integrity of the vote, Prasna points to an 'invisible hand' that has influenced the integrity in eight different ways. I will leave it at that.

For those hoping for military intervention, know that the military will not intervene unless public unrest surpasses levels far beyond what we is being witnessed post-elections. There might also be a change in the military leadership before it can happen. In addition, note that the last time this happened it had the full support of the honorable US government. The honorable President Lula Da Silva currently enjoys the support of nearly the entire international community. While the military might be inclined and even able to intervene, they cannot do so unless the public unrest crosses a critical threshold. They however appear to be planning carefully and waiting patiently for the right moment. None of this can be rushed or else failure is certain. This is because a coup is successful only when there is an element of surprise and it is well planned/executed. They cannot do it now when everyone might expect this and the entire international community stands ready to isolate Brazil in that scenario. The aforementioned threshold of public unrest is likely to be breached in late 2023 and early 2024. And it might take until July 2024 for the military to step in. And when the time comes, it will be very risky operation but surprisingly successful. Patience and preparation appears to be key.

For those of you who have the means to escape the unrest on the horizon in Brazil, I would counsel you to consider the option and do so before Feb 21, 2023 if possible.

I hope I've been of service to you. God bless us all.

Given the hate and racial tensions Indians are experiencing on social media, let's not be surprised if blame is placed on the honorable Indian pilot from ISRO.

As you can see, this is not a risk worth taking given the human lives involved and the potential impact to the legendary reputation of the Falcon 9 rocket. The backup launch time of 7:37 AM ET on Thursday, June 12 isn't ideal either; I'll consider it only after this upcoming window has passed.

records.title: GSLV Mk II / NISAR — T-0 No-Go, T-16d
records.claim:
Muhūrtha Risk Advisory & Open Challenge
Mission: NASA–ISRO SAR (NISAR)
Vehicle: GSLV Mk II
Pad: Satish Dhawan Space Centre, Sriharikota
Published T-0: Wednesday, 16 July 2025 | 17:00 IST

I. Consolidated Forecast

Pilgrimages to Sri Venkateswara at the Tirumala Tirupati @TTDevasthanams hill shrine—often paired with a stop at Chengalamma Temple in Sullurpeta—are woven into @isro culture. Engineers regularly present miniature launch vehicles, seeking blessings for clean engine starts and perfect staging. The ritual never conflicts with calculus or telemetry; it complements them.

Yet a contradiction emerges: the temple visit is welcomed, but the astrological timing counsel that flows from the same tradition is set aside. That selective respect will prove costly. If this advisory is ignored—if the analysis is dismissed or mocked—no procession up Tirumala's 3,550 steps will rescue the mission. The GSLV Mk II will not lift off at 17:00 IST. A countdown hold, scrub, and re-schedule stand at Probability = 1.0 (certain).

Should the team nevertheless force a liftoff simply to prove this forecast wrong, the flight will be non-nominal. Guidance faults will arise, orbit insertion will miss the mark, and the result will be the loss of the launch vehicle and/or the $1.5 billion satellite. Even partial survival would leave @NASA @isro @NASAJPL NISAR unable to meet its science objectives.

Below are the only fault paths highlighted to date—no more, no less. Probabilities are attached; each is now rated High.

🪫 Electrical Power-System Undervolt | Probability ≈ 0.80 (High)
Weeks on the pad let individual battery cells drift. At least four minutes before liftoff (T-4 min) the avionics self-check will detect low voltage on the 28-volt bus and command an automatic hold.

🌡️ Cryogenic Fuel-Temperature Imbalance | Probability ≈ 0.80 (High)
Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) cooled even 0.8 K too far can turn slushy, while liquid oxygen (LOX) may stratify. Engines will read the wrong mixture ratio and shut the start sequence within milliseconds.

🔥 Ignition-Sequence Mistiming | Probability ≈ 0.80 (High)
The hypergolic igniter pulse and main-valve opening must align within ~30 ms. Any slip will prompt the controller to abort, avoiding a hard-start over-pressure event.

🌬️ Vent & Pressurization-Valve Stiction | Probability ≈ 0.80 (High)
Ice collars can lock a gaseous-nitrogen (GN₂) vent or a helium pressurization valve. Tank pressure will drift outside limits; flight software will veto continuation.

🛰️ Guidance & Sun-Synchronous-Orbit Injection Error | Probability ≈ 0.80 (High)
If launch proceeds regardless, inertial-reference drift and star-sensor dropout will corrupt guidance. The upper-stage burn will significantly miss the required sun-synchronous-orbit plane. Recovery ΔV will exceed onboard propellant, dooming the satellite to an unusable orbit or outright loss.

II. Courteous—Yet Firm—Challenge
1⃣ Hold the published T-0—16 July 2025, 17:00 IST—exactly.
2⃣ Run the entire countdown unchanged; do not tweak a single step to appease this forecast.
3⃣ When the attempt ends, release the raw data—battery voltages, ignition-timing traces, valve positions, guidance logs, orbit vectors, and anomaly flags—so anyone can judge the outcome.

If the rocket lifts on time and flies clean, engineering excellence will earn full credit. If the sequence halts or the flight stumbles—as predicted—the record will show that astrological timing counsel deserves the same respect already afforded to the Tirumala Tirupati pilgrimage.

records.title: GSLV-F16 / NISAR — Slip & Orbital-Deviation Class
records.claim:
Muhūrtha Risk Advisory
Mission: NASA–ISRO SAR (NISAR)
Agencies: @nasa @nasajpl @isro
Vehicle: GSLV Mk II
Pad: Satish Dhawan Space Centre, Sriharikota
Published T-0: Wednesday, 30 July 2025 | 17:00 IST

I. Astrological Advisory
The launch window at 17:00 IST on July 30, 2025, is flagged as highly unfavorable and inauspicious. The following forecast outlines the most likely failure modes. Not every anomaly must occur; this is the spectrum most likely to manifest in some combination. Based on these failure modes, proceeding with the launch is not a risk worth taking with a $1.5 billion investment—indeed, it would be reckless.

II. Spectrum of Failure Modes

🗓️ Schedule Slip — Probability: 90%
Although we will proceed with the launch countdown this time, a punctual liftoff at 17:00 IST remains highly unlikely. Meteorological factors, vehicle issues, and range constraints are expected to cause holds, delays, scrubs, or rescheduling. Notably, high-altitude winds—jet stream winds at upper atmospheric levels—can generate turbulence that destabilizes the rocket during critical ascent phases. These winds can lead to guidance errors, structural oscillations, or oscillatory stress, which may require reprogramming of guidance systems or cause launch delays. In extreme cases, they could necessitate holding the launch until conditions improve. Ignoring these warnings risks a watery demise or uncontrolled explosion. A similar schedule slip advisory was issued for the launch of the @blueorigin New Shepard NS-24 rocket, owned by @jeffbezos. The launch was first put on hold due to cold launch-site temperatures and subsequently scrubbed due to issues with ground systems.
Evidence: https://x.com/vijayjyotishusa/status/1824307635103076432

🔥 Thermal Excursions & Explosion — Probability: 90%
Thermal excursions during ascent, MECO, and separation are expected. Although the GSLV's fuels are of exceptional quality—usually an advantage—breaching thermal thresholds can turn this strength into a significant risk. Reaching these critical temperatures could ignite the fuels, leading to thermal runaway, explosive failure, or rapid disassembly. The combination of thermal stress and potential guidance or control system failures greatly increases the likelihood of total failure. A similar thermal excursion warning was issued for the launch of @SpaceX Starship 2 and shared with @elonmusk. Unfortunately, it was not considered, resulting in a catastrophic explosion of the LOX tank.
Evidence: https://x.com/vijayjyotishusa/status/1824307998422179901

🚩 Orbit Deviations & Deployment Failures — Probability: 80%
Guidance anomalies during separation or burns could cause the satellite to drift from its planned sun-synchronous orbit. Such deviations impair instrument activation and orientation, risking premature reentry or total loss of control. A similar orbital insertion anomaly advisory was issued for the launch of the @nasa @astrobotic Lunar Lander 'Peregrine,' which flew on the @ulalaunch Vulcan rocket. Despite extensive outreach, including to @ulalaunch CEO @torybruno (who is very approachable and I like him!), the advisory was not considered for various technical and business reasons, and the launch proceeded as scheduled. Consequently, the predicted anomalies occurred during orbital insertion, resulting in the complete loss of the lunar lander Peregrine.
Evidence: https://x.com/vijayjyotishusa/status/1824307269741449356

🔴 Loss of SAR Imaging — Probability: 90%
Severe guidance or orbit errors will compromise the satellite's imaging capabilities, preventing its radar antenna from maintaining proper orientation or stability. As a result, high-resolution imaging of Earth's surface will be impossible, rendering the payload either partially or entirely ineffective, resulting in mission failure.

📡 Communication Failures — Probability: 80%
System errors and particularly—software bugs—may cause loss of contact with ground stations, preventing timely guidance corrections or abort commands. This can lead to uncontrolled drift, reentry, or mission shutdown, with no way to intervene.

III. Standing Offer for Collaboration
I am ready to suggest an astrologically favorable alternative that satisfies all range, orbit, and payload requirements. Our shared goal remains: a flawless NISAR mission that advances science for all. The decision rests with the launch team.

records.title: Starship Flight 13 — Twelve-Call Flight Review Sealed Before the Window · Scrub Recommendation · Starlink V3 Payload-Shortfall Headline
records.claim:
STARSHIP FLIGHT 13 — FLIGHT REVIEW & GO/NO-GO ASSESSMENT · SEALED BEFORE THE WINDOW

Vehicles: Super Heavy Booster 20 + Ship 40 (V3 architecture, Raptor 3), Starbase Pad 2. Profile: suborbital, ~65 min — hot-stage separation, booster boostback + soft Gulf splashdown, Ship SECO → Starlink V3 deploy (20 operational satellites, 6 camera-equipped) → single-Raptor vacuum relight → entry interface → controlled Indian Ocean splashdown. This is the first Starship flight carrying functional Starlink V3 satellites rather than mass simulators. Cargo consequence and programmatic precedent are materially higher than on any prior flight.

The committed probability envelope, sealed now and never revised: every graded call below carries a minimum probability of 0.78 — likely. Within the shifted regime from ≈T+6 to ≈T+11 minutes, every called class rises to 0.90 — very likely, returning to 0.78 thereafter. These expectations stand despite all remedies undertaken after the 16 July abort, including the two-engine replacement.

Twelve graded calls are marked ▶. Each grades after the flight from public evidence — webcast, SpaceX statements, independent tracking — regardless of outcome, misses kept at full weight. Together they are one described timeline, to be laid over the actual flight phase by phase and graded in place. Scope: Call 1 is specific to the 20 July window; all flight calls attach to Flight 13 whenever it flies, through 3 August 2026.

2/ — PRE-LAUNCH / PROPELLANT LOAD THROUGH T-0

Filter/blockage or ice formation (cryo LOX/CH4 lines, engine inlet screens, chill-down orifices): first signature during engine chill (T-21:30) as elevated ΔP across inlet filters or anomalous chill-down mass-flow rates; progressive occlusion develops as temperatures fall; tank ullage pressure rise lags command. Autonomous logic inhibits engine start if any single-engine ΔP exceeds redline. Seal/gasket extrusion or micro-leak (tank domes, feedline flanges, QD interfaces, gimbal bellows): continuous LOX/CH4 concentration rise on pad sensors, localized temperature depression, ullage decay beyond predicted boil-off — detectable during load (T-37 to T-2), automatic scrub if concentrations cross explosive-limit thresholds. Loose fitting or clamp fretting: acoustic emission and intermittent manifold pressure oscillations during chill-down flow. Valve position or actuation failure: commanded-vs-reported mismatch; stretched Pc rise times; the flight computer aborts within 200–400 ms of missing chamber-pressure rise at the T-3 s start sequence. This exact class occurred on 16 July — multiple booster Raptors failed to ignite at T-0 — and two engines are being replaced. The elevated concern at this window: an abrupt discontinuity or incomplete transition from start command to stable multi-engine thrust — sudden intensification of latent lag into full non-ignition or asymmetric light-up.

▶ CALL 1 — p ≥ 0.78 (20 July window only; grades only if no liftoff): if the 20 July window does not achieve liftoff, the proximate cause is the propulsion ignition/actuation class — not weather, not range, not other ground systems. Graded on SpaceX's stated cause.

▶ CALL 2 — p ≥ 0.78: even if the count proceeds, the start-to-stable-thrust transition exhibits an observable anomaly — delayed or asymmetric light-up, or at least one engine failing to start or dropping out within the first ten seconds. Graded on the webcast engine display and commentary.

3/ — LIFTOFF → MAX-Q → MECO (T+0 to T+2:18)

Blockage or cavitation: individual Raptor Pc drop of 5–15% within the first seconds, O/F shift, thrust-vector margin reduction, early engine-out below 80% of commanded Pc. Seal failure under ascent loads: rapid local temperature rise once hot gas or cryogen escapes; at Max-Q (T+0:58) external dynamic pressure combined with an internal leak can produce progressive structural heating or secondary fire. Loose fittings: fretting under 3–5 g axial plus vibration; step-change manifold pressure when a joint opens; secondary cascade into adjacent lines is the non-nominal risk. Valve anomaly: discrete or stretched steps rather than gradual degradation — lag under rising demand is the elevated concern. Mixture excursion: vehicle-level performance shortfall at MECO with elevated turbine temperatures on affected engines.

▶ CALL 3 — p ≥ 0.78: at least one booster Raptor exhibits a publicly observable performance anomaly during ascent — an engine out on the webcast display, or an ascent propulsion anomaly acknowledged by SpaceX.

4/ — HOT-STAGING / SEPARATION (T+2:18–2:25) — HIGHEST SINGLE-POINT SENSITIVITY

Ship Raptors must achieve full Pc while booster residual thrust is present. Failed, delayed, or progressive Ship valve opening produces incomplete or asymmetric thrust at separation — relative-velocity error, potential re-contact, or excessive tip-off rates. Seal or fitting failure in the interstage region is immediately exposed to the booster plume; aft-skirt sensors register within 100–300 ms. Elevated risk of an abrupt or incomplete phase transition — a sudden discontinuity in the thrust hand-over that does not cleanly complete the staging sequence.

▶ CALL 4 — p ≥ 0.78: the staging sequence exhibits an observable abrupt-or-incomplete-transition signature — one or more of the six Ship engines lighting late or not at all, visibly asymmetric light-up, excessive tip-off, or a re-contact-class event. Graded on webcast and SpaceX statements.

5/ — BOOSTER BOOSTBACK + LANDING BURN, AND THE SHIFTED REGIME (T+2:25 → ≈T+11)

Boostback relight failure: missing or lagged Pc rise on one or more commanded engines within 300–500 ms of start command; progressive restriction from earlier chill or load stretches rise times further; grid-fin authority cannot fully compensate the ΔV shortfall — downrange error grows rapidly. Mixture or contamination: combustion instability (Pc oscillation above 5–8% peak-to-peak) or hard-start spikes; turbine inlet temperature exceedance and early shutdown. Seal or fitting leak during the high-thrust landing burn reproduces the ascent cascade at low altitude with no recovery margin.

From approximately T+6 the vehicle operates under a shifted foundational and control regime. This regime places elevated stress on structural and foundational stability, intensifies mechanical friction and obstruction-type failure modes, and links overall vehicle continuity to sudden or incomplete outcomes. It is active throughout the landing burn (T+6:27–6:53), Ship SECO (T+8:05), and early coast to approximately T+11 minutes. Any progressive restriction or latent valve lag carries elevated probability of sudden intensification into full non-ignition, asymmetric light-up, or incomplete thrust ramp at the moment the landing burn is commanded.

▶ CALL 5 — p ≥ 0.78 (boostback, ~T+2:30): at least one commanded boostback engine fails or lags relight, or the boostback burn ends early. Graded on webcast and SpaceX statements.

▶ CALL 6 — p ≥ 0.90 (landing burn, T+6:27–6:53 — inside the regime): clean booster recovery does not occur — missing or lagged landing-burn start, engine shortfall during the burn, or a hard or destructive water impact in place of a soft, controlled Gulf splashdown.

▶ CALL 7 — p ≥ 0.90 (the regime window, T+6 → T+11): within this window, at least one sudden or incomplete anomaly beyond nominal manifests on either vehicle — terminal booster events, SECO irregularity, early-coast attitude or propellant-state excursion, or any anomaly in this window acknowledged by SpaceX.

▶ CALL 8 — p ≥ 0.90 (SECO, T+8:05 — inside the regime): Ship SECO or the achieved energy state is off-nominal — materially early or late cutoff, or an underperformance callout.

6/ — SHIP COAST → PAYLOAD DEPLOY → IN-SPACE RELIGHT (T+8:05 → ~T+39)

Residual seal leak produces excess ullage-pressure decay after SECO beyond predicted radiative/conductive boil-off; propellant settling for the relight is compromised and free-surface dynamics become unpredictable. Valve or blockage on the single vacuum Raptor selected for relight: failure to achieve Pc, or stretched rise time, within the commanded window is unambiguous — progressive restriction accumulated from earlier phases is the higher-probability subset. Post-coast O/F excursion or residual stratification becomes visible only at the relight command. Sensor disagreement that remains inside yellow limits until the discrete command is the non-nominal masking path. Payload deploy (T+16:40–27:39) is the first attempt with functional Starlink V3 hardware: any residual attitude error, propellant-state anomaly, or power/thermal excursion inherited from earlier phases directly threatens stack release, solar-array deployment, and the brief connectivity demonstration. Six camera-equipped satellites will still attempt heat-shield imaging if vehicle rates remain inside limits, but the primary cargo objective is at elevated risk. Under the regime active from ≈T+6, residual anomalies from the early high-intensity window enter an environment that favors sudden transformation, incomplete recovery, and compromised foundational stability.

▶ CALL 9 — p ≥ 0.78 across the flight; p ≥ 0.90 inside T+6→T+11: observable degradation of telemetry or link integrity — sustained (≥30 s) loss of vehicle data on the official stream, acknowledged dropouts or frequency instability, or delayed acquisition — beyond momentary blips. Graded on the public stream itself and SpaceX commentary.

▶ CALL 10 — p ≥ 0.78 — THE HEADLINE (this advisory's probability at seal): the functional Starlink V3 payload objective falls short in whole or in part — not all 20 satellites released, with solar arrays deployed and the connectivity demonstration confirmed. This would be the program's first operational payload failure. Graded on SpaceX statements and webcast confirmation.

▶ CALL 11 — p ≥ 0.78 (vacuum relight, ~T+39): the single-Raptor relight objective is not fully achieved — skipped, failed, or degraded.

7/ — ENTRY INTERFACE → LANDING BURN (T+47:30 ONWARD)

Any residual propellant from an earlier leak becomes an entry heating and fire risk once aero-thermal loads begin. The final landing-burn engine start is subject to the same valve/blockage/ignition classes, now with possible progressive restriction carried forward; engine-out logic will be exercised; hard-splashdown probability rises sharply if Pc is not achieved on the final engine. Secondary cascade into attitude control or residual-propellant fire is the non-nominal concern.

▶ CALL 12 — p ≥ 0.78: the controlled-splashdown objective is degraded — a final-burn engine anomaly, hard impact, or breakup in place of the nominal flip and soft splashdown.

8/ — DETECTION HIERARCHY & RISK CONCENTRATIONS

Primary: high-rate Pc, O/F, valve-position, and ΔP sensors (10–100 Hz). Secondary: structural thermocouples, accelerometers, ullage pressure. Tertiary: ground optical and pad concentration sensors (pre-flight only). Autonomous abort and engine-out logic operate inside 200–500 ms windows; progressive lag and sensor disagreement push detection toward the upper end. Concurrent telemetry or frequency-path degradation compounds every other class by reducing observability precisely when progressive or non-nominal behavior is most likely to appear. The 16 July abort demonstrated the T-0 redline logic functioning exactly as designed.

Bottom-line risk concentrations, full timeline ordered: (1) T-0 ignition — abrupt discontinuity or incomplete transition from start command to stable thrust; (2) hot-staging separation dynamics; (3) the booster landing burn (T+6:27–6:53) inside a prolonged high-intensity regime extending to ≈T+11; (4) the shifted regime from ≈T+6 — stressing foundational stability, intensifying friction- and obstruction-type failures, linking continuity to sudden or incomplete outcomes — active through landing burn, SECO, early coast, and the approach to payload deploy; (5) Ship vacuum relight and functional Starlink V3 deploy preparation; (6) seal/fitting integrity under Max-Q and hot-staging loads. Elevated subsets throughout: progressive restriction, secondary non-nominal cascades, and post-coast propellant-condition or O/F excursions.

9/ — FINAL RECOMMENDATION

Scrub. SpaceX should consider deferring this window pending public confirmation of root cause beyond the two-engine replacement — including any common-mode contribution to abrupt or incomplete transitions across the extended early-flight window — and further verification of telemetry link margin, frequency stability, and vehicle response across the full T-0-to-mid-coast regime, including the characteristics that change near T+6. The combination of a prolonged early-sequence high-intensity period, a mid-flight regime that favors sudden or incomplete outcomes, progressive propulsion classes, reduced observability, and first-flight operational cargo makes the downside asymmetric. A deferral is preferable to a high likelihood of partial or total loss of the 20 operational Starlink V3 satellites on a test flight that can still be deferred.

10/ — THE RECORD'S TERMS

Twelve calls, one described timeline, sealed before the window and graded after from public evidence — each call in place on the flight's own clock, misses kept at full weight, as with all 103 seals before this one. companions.title: Starship Flight 13 — Twelve-Call Flight Review Sealed Before the Window · Scrub Recommendation · Starlink V3 Payload-Shortfall Headline · companion seal
companions.claim:
STARSHIP FLIGHT 13 — FLIGHT REVIEW & GO/NO-GO ASSESSMENT · SEALED BEFORE THE WINDOW

Vehicles: Super Heavy Booster 20 + Ship 40 (V3 architecture, Raptor 3), Starbase Pad 2. Profile: suborbital, ~65 min — hot-stage separation, booster boostback + soft Gulf splashdown, Ship SECO → Starlink V3 deploy (20 operational satellites, 6 camera-equipped) → single-Raptor vacuum relight → entry interface → controlled Indian Ocean splashdown. This is the first Starship flight carrying functional Starlink V3 satellites rather than mass simulators. Cargo consequence and programmatic precedent are materially higher than on any prior flight.

The committed probability envelope, sealed now and never revised: every graded call below carries a minimum probability of 0.78 — likely. Within the shifted regime from ≈T+6 to ≈T+11 minutes, every called class rises to 0.90 — very likely, returning to 0.78 thereafter. These expectations stand despite all remedies undertaken after the 16 July abort, including the two-engine replacement.

Twelve graded calls are marked ▶. Each grades after the flight from public evidence — webcast, SpaceX statements, independent tracking — regardless of outcome, misses kept at full weight. Together they are one described timeline, to be laid over the actual flight phase by phase and graded in place. Scope: Call 1 is specific to the 20 July window; all flight calls attach to Flight 13 whenever it flies, through 3 August 2026.

2/ — PRE-LAUNCH / PROPELLANT LOAD THROUGH T-0

Filter/blockage or ice formation (cryo LOX/CH4 lines, engine inlet screens, chill-down orifices): first signature during engine chill (T-21:30) as elevated ΔP across inlet filters or anomalous chill-down mass-flow rates; progressive occlusion develops as temperatures fall; tank ullage pressure rise lags command. Autonomous logic inhibits engine start if any single-engine ΔP exceeds redline. Seal/gasket extrusion or micro-leak (tank domes, feedline flanges, QD interfaces, gimbal bellows): continuous LOX/CH4 concentration rise on pad sensors, localized temperature depression, ullage decay beyond predicted boil-off — detectable during load (T-37 to T-2), automatic scrub if concentrations cross explosive-limit thresholds. Loose fitting or clamp fretting: acoustic emission and intermittent manifold pressure oscillations during chill-down flow. Valve position or actuation failure: commanded-vs-reported mismatch; stretched Pc rise times; the flight computer aborts within 200–400 ms of missing chamber-pressure rise at the T-3 s start sequence. This exact class occurred on 16 July — multiple booster Raptors failed to ignite at T-0 — and two engines are being replaced. The elevated concern at this window: an abrupt discontinuity or incomplete transition from start command to stable multi-engine thrust — sudden intensification of latent lag into full non-ignition or asymmetric light-up.

▶ CALL 1 — p ≥ 0.78 (20 July window only; grades only if no liftoff): if the 20 July window does not achieve liftoff, the proximate cause is the propulsion ignition/actuation class — not weather, not range, not other ground systems. Graded on SpaceX's stated cause.

▶ CALL 2 — p ≥ 0.78: even if the count proceeds, the start-to-stable-thrust transition exhibits an observable anomaly — delayed or asymmetric light-up, or at least one engine failing to start or dropping out within the first ten seconds. Graded on the webcast engine display and commentary.

3/ — LIFTOFF → MAX-Q → MECO (T+0 to T+2:18)

Blockage or cavitation: individual Raptor Pc drop of 5–15% within the first seconds, O/F shift, thrust-vector margin reduction, early engine-out below 80% of commanded Pc. Seal failure under ascent loads: rapid local temperature rise once hot gas or cryogen escapes; at Max-Q (T+0:58) external dynamic pressure combined with an internal leak can produce progressive structural heating or secondary fire. Loose fittings: fretting under 3–5 g axial plus vibration; step-change manifold pressure when a joint opens; secondary cascade into adjacent lines is the non-nominal risk. Valve anomaly: discrete or stretched steps rather than gradual degradation — lag under rising demand is the elevated concern. Mixture excursion: vehicle-level performance shortfall at MECO with elevated turbine temperatures on affected engines.

▶ CALL 3 — p ≥ 0.78: at least one booster Raptor exhibits a publicly observable performance anomaly during ascent — an engine out on the webcast display, or an ascent propulsion anomaly acknowledged by SpaceX.

4/ — HOT-STAGING / SEPARATION (T+2:18–2:25) — HIGHEST SINGLE-POINT SENSITIVITY

Ship Raptors must achieve full Pc while booster residual thrust is present. Failed, delayed, or progressive Ship valve opening produces incomplete or asymmetric thrust at separation — relative-velocity error, potential re-contact, or excessive tip-off rates. Seal or fitting failure in the interstage region is immediately exposed to the booster plume; aft-skirt sensors register within 100–300 ms. Elevated risk of an abrupt or incomplete phase transition — a sudden discontinuity in the thrust hand-over that does not cleanly complete the staging sequence.

▶ CALL 4 — p ≥ 0.78: the staging sequence exhibits an observable abrupt-or-incomplete-transition signature — one or more of the six Ship engines lighting late or not at all, visibly asymmetric light-up, excessive tip-off, or a re-contact-class event. Graded on webcast and SpaceX statements.

5/ — BOOSTER BOOSTBACK + LANDING BURN, AND THE SHIFTED REGIME (T+2:25 → ≈T+11)

Boostback relight failure: missing or lagged Pc rise on one or more commanded engines within 300–500 ms of start command; progressive restriction from earlier chill or load stretches rise times further; grid-fin authority cannot fully compensate the ΔV shortfall — downrange error grows rapidly. Mixture or contamination: combustion instability (Pc oscillation above 5–8% peak-to-peak) or hard-start spikes; turbine inlet temperature exceedance and early shutdown. Seal or fitting leak during the high-thrust landing burn reproduces the ascent cascade at low altitude with no recovery margin.

From approximately T+6 the vehicle operates under a shifted foundational and control regime. This regime places elevated stress on structural and foundational stability, intensifies mechanical friction and obstruction-type failure modes, and links overall vehicle continuity to sudden or incomplete outcomes. It is active throughout the landing burn (T+6:27–6:53), Ship SECO (T+8:05), and early coast to approximately T+11 minutes. Any progressive restriction or latent valve lag carries elevated probability of sudden intensification into full non-ignition, asymmetric light-up, or incomplete thrust ramp at the moment the landing burn is commanded.

▶ CALL 5 — p ≥ 0.78 (boostback, ~T+2:30): at least one commanded boostback engine fails or lags relight, or the boostback burn ends early. Graded on webcast and SpaceX statements.

▶ CALL 6 — p ≥ 0.90 (landing burn, T+6:27–6:53 — inside the regime): clean booster recovery does not occur — missing or lagged landing-burn start, engine shortfall during the burn, or a hard or destructive water impact in place of a soft, controlled Gulf splashdown.

▶ CALL 7 — p ≥ 0.90 (the regime window, T+6 → T+11): within this window, at least one sudden or incomplete anomaly beyond nominal manifests on either vehicle — terminal booster events, SECO irregularity, early-coast attitude or propellant-state excursion, or any anomaly in this window acknowledged by SpaceX.

▶ CALL 8 — p ≥ 0.90 (SECO, T+8:05 — inside the regime): Ship SECO or the achieved energy state is off-nominal — materially early or late cutoff, or an underperformance callout.

6/ — SHIP COAST → PAYLOAD DEPLOY → IN-SPACE RELIGHT (T+8:05 → ~T+39)

Residual seal leak produces excess ullage-pressure decay after SECO beyond predicted radiative/conductive boil-off; propellant settling for the relight is compromised and free-surface dynamics become unpredictable. Valve or blockage on the single vacuum Raptor selected for relight: failure to achieve Pc, or stretched rise time, within the commanded window is unambiguous — progressive restriction accumulated from earlier phases is the higher-probability subset. Post-coast O/F excursion or residual stratification becomes visible only at the relight command. Sensor disagreement that remains inside yellow limits until the discrete command is the non-nominal masking path. Payload deploy (T+16:40–27:39) is the first attempt with functional Starlink V3 hardware: any residual attitude error, propellant-state anomaly, or power/thermal excursion inherited from earlier phases directly threatens stack release, solar-array deployment, and the brief connectivity demonstration. Six camera-equipped satellites will still attempt heat-shield imaging if vehicle rates remain inside limits, but the primary cargo objective is at elevated risk. Under the regime active from ≈T+6, residual anomalies from the early high-intensity window enter an environment that favors sudden transformation, incomplete recovery, and compromised foundational stability.

▶ CALL 9 — p ≥ 0.78 across the flight; p ≥ 0.90 inside T+6→T+11: observable degradation of telemetry or link integrity — sustained (≥30 s) loss of vehicle data on the official stream, acknowledged dropouts or frequency instability, or delayed acquisition — beyond momentary blips. Graded on the public stream itself and SpaceX commentary.

▶ CALL 10 — p ≥ 0.78 — THE HEADLINE (this advisory's probability at seal): the functional Starlink V3 payload objective falls short in whole or in part — not all 20 satellites released, with solar arrays deployed and the connectivity demonstration confirmed. This would be the program's first operational payload failure. Graded on SpaceX statements and webcast confirmation.

▶ CALL 11 — p ≥ 0.78 (vacuum relight, ~T+39): the single-Raptor relight objective is not fully achieved — skipped, failed, or degraded.

7/ — ENTRY INTERFACE → LANDING BURN (T+47:30 ONWARD)

Any residual propellant from an earlier leak becomes an entry heating and fire risk once aero-thermal loads begin. The final landing-burn engine start is subject to the same valve/blockage/ignition classes, now with possible progressive restriction carried forward; engine-out logic will be exercised; hard-splashdown probability rises sharply if Pc is not achieved on the final engine. Secondary cascade into attitude control or residual-propellant fire is the non-nominal concern.

▶ CALL 12 — p ≥ 0.78: the controlled-splashdown objective is degraded — a final-burn engine anomaly, hard impact, or breakup in place of the nominal flip and soft splashdown.

8/ — DETECTION HIERARCHY & RISK CONCENTRATIONS

Primary: high-rate Pc, O/F, valve-position, and ΔP sensors (10–100 Hz). Secondary: structural thermocouples, accelerometers, ullage pressure. Tertiary: ground optical and pad concentration sensors (pre-flight only). Autonomous abort and engine-out logic operate inside 200–500 ms windows; progressive lag and sensor disagreement push detection toward the upper end. Concurrent telemetry or frequency-path degradation compounds every other class by reducing observability precisely when progressive or non-nominal behavior is most likely to appear. The 16 July abort demonstrated the T-0 redline logic functioning exactly as designed.

Bottom-line risk concentrations, full timeline ordered: (1) T-0 ignition — abrupt discontinuity or incomplete transition from start command to stable thrust; (2) hot-staging separation dynamics; (3) the booster landing burn (T+6:27–6:53) inside a prolonged high-intensity regime extending to ≈T+11; (4) the shifted regime from ≈T+6 — stressing foundational stability, intensifying friction- and obstruction-type failures, linking continuity to sudden or incomplete outcomes — active through landing burn, SECO, early coast, and the approach to payload deploy; (5) Ship vacuum relight and functional Starlink V3 deploy preparation; (6) seal/fitting integrity under Max-Q and hot-staging loads. Elevated subsets throughout: progressive restriction, secondary non-nominal cascades, and post-coast propellant-condition or O/F excursions.

9/ — FINAL RECOMMENDATION

Scrub. SpaceX should consider deferring this window pending public confirmation of root cause beyond the two-engine replacement — including any common-mode contribution to abrupt or incomplete transitions across the extended early-flight window — and further verification of telemetry link margin, frequency stability, and vehicle response across the full T-0-to-mid-coast regime, including the characteristics that change near T+6. The combination of a prolonged early-sequence high-intensity period, a mid-flight regime that favors sudden or incomplete outcomes, progressive propulsion classes, reduced observability, and first-flight operational cargo makes the downside asymmetric. A deferral is preferable to a high likelihood of partial or total loss of the 20 operational Starlink V3 satellites on a test flight that can still be deferred.

10/ — THE RECORD'S TERMS

Twelve calls, one described timeline, sealed before the window and graded after from public evidence — each call in place on the flight's own clock, misses kept at full weight, as with all 103 seals before this one. The compounded improbability of this description is priced at seal by the record's published 1-in-N engine — partition floor, capped multipliers, correlated components discounted, never naively multiplied — and shown on the advisory page. This assessment is opinion derived from a disclosed, unconventional timing methodology; it rests on no non-public information and asserts no fact about any company's internal knowledge or condition; it addresses a mission and its day, never any security. No-Position attestation as of the seal date, full grading rubric, and the frozen consensus snapshot: on this advisory page. The SHA-256 of this text enters the Bitcoin-anchored manifest at seal.

ADDENDUM 1 TO LA-023 — SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · COMMON-MODE CONTAMINATION HYPOTHESIS

The assessment sealed 17 July identified, phase by phase, the anomaly classes expected on Flight 13. This addendum commits — in advance, gradeable like everything else — to a causal hypothesis beneath them.

THE HYPOTHESIS: the 16 July T-0 multi-engine non-ignition and the anomaly classes named in the sealed assessment share a common-mode origin — trace contamination or non-propellant foreign material within the propellant feed, engine inlet, injector, or valve interfaces of the integrated vehicle, of a kind that engine replacement alone does not clear, because the plausible introduction paths (shared conditioning, manifolds, filters, ground-support interfaces during recent ground operations) are common to engines that were not replaced, on both stages. Stated plainly: the two-engine replacement addresses the hardware that failed on 16 July; it does not, on the public record available before this window, demonstrably clear the system.

This is a hypothesis derived from the record's disclosed, unconventional timing methodology and from public information only. It asserts no engineering fact, imputes no negligence, no contamination-control failure, and no knowledge to SpaceX or any person; SpaceX's own verification processes may well have already excluded it — the public record before the window simply does not show that. It addresses the mission and the day, never any security.

Three graded calls, marked ▶A, sealed under the same discipline as the original twelve — public evidence only, misses kept at full weight:

▶ CALL A1 — p ≥ 0.78 — ROOT-CAUSE CLASS (event-anchored: grades on the FIRST qualifying public disclosure of cause — a SpaceX published cause statement, or the closure findings of an FAA-overseen mishap investigation into any Flight-13 mishap, whichever comes first): the disclosed cause set includes a contamination / foreign-material / residue / propellant-conditioning / fluid-system-cleanliness / shared-systems (common-mode) factor — not solely an isolated single-engine-unit defect. Graded on the disclosure's words, verbatim. Pre-committed: the 16 July pad abort alone triggers no FAA report, and technical root causes are rarely detailed outside regulatory findings — so if no qualifying disclosure exists within 90 days of the Flight-13 flight (or, if it never flies, within 90 days of this seal), this call is VOID — recorded as ungradeable, never counted as a hit or a miss.

▶ CALL A2 — p ≥ 0.78 — CROSS-SYSTEM EXPRESSION (whole flight): propulsion-class anomalies manifest on BOTH stages — at least one booster propulsion anomaly AND at least one Ship propulsion-or-propellant-state anomaly — the two-sided pattern a common-mode origin predicts and an isolated-engine-defect explanation does not. Graded on webcast, SpaceX statements, independent tracking.

▶ CALL A3 — p ≥ 0.78 — THE SWAP IS NOT THE CURE (through flight, and statements through 31 August 2026): the two-engine replacement proves insufficient as the complete remedy, evidenced by at least one of: (i) additional propulsion inspections, tests, or a cause-related window delay beyond the swap publicly reported before liftoff; (ii) the vehicle flies and at least one propulsion-class anomaly from the sealed assessment still manifests; (iii) a post-flight SpaceX statement or official/regulatory disclosure identifies remaining or system-level (not replaced-unit) contributing factors. Pre-committed: a clean flight with no such statement and no such delay grades this call a MISS at full weight.

RECOMMENDATION (unchanged, reinforced): scrub — with the investigative focus this hypothesis makes concrete: verification of propellant-wetted paths and shared conditioning equipment across the FULL engine set of both stages, not the replaced units alone.

Terms: opinion from a disclosed methodology; no non-public information; no assertion about any company's internal knowledge or condition; never revised after seal. The SHA-256 of this addendum enters the record as a companion seal to LA-023 — the original 17 July seal and its twelve calls are unchanged and unchangeable.

ADDENDUM 2 TO LA-023 — SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · ENERGETIC IGNITION TRANSIENT HYPOTHESIS

Addendum 1 committed to a common-mode contamination origin — why ignition and flow performance may degrade. This addendum commits to the adjacent failure character: how a degraded or altered ignition event can amplify rather than merely fail.

THE HYPOTHESIS: during an engine start sequence, residual material, altered surface conditions, or off-nominal propellant state at the injector and ignition zone produces a harder, more energetic ignition transient than modeled — localized pressure spikes, incomplete or uneven atomization, secondary exothermic reactions — with the potential to transition into sustained combustion instability or rapid energy release within the engine or immediate aft compartment, including interaction between engines during a simultaneous multi-engine start, where one engine's anomalous ignition products affect adjacent engines' inlet conditions or flame stability. This is distinct from simple failure to ignite: ignition occurs, but departs from controlled combustion. The 16 July event demonstrated that multiple engines can fail to reach stable ignition together; this addendum addresses the possibility that a start transient is MORE energetic than expected rather than absent. The risk carries elevated consequence at every relight — boostback, landing burn, and above all the single-Raptor vacuum relight on Ship 40, where back-pressure and thermal environment differ from ground and ascent conditions — and within the shifted regime from approximately T+6, where an energetic event has shorter time constants and less recovery margin than a gradual performance loss.

This is a hypothesis derived from the record's disclosed, unconventional timing methodology and from public information only. It asserts no engineering fact, imputes no negligence and no knowledge to SpaceX or any person; SpaceX's own margins and test data may well already bound it — the public record before the window does not show that. It addresses the mission and the day, never any security.

Two graded calls, sealed under the same discipline — public evidence only, misses kept at full weight:

▶ CALL A4 — p ≥ 0.78 — ENERGETIC START TRANSIENT (any commanded engine start: T-0, boostback, landing burn, vacuum relight, final burn): at least one start event exhibits an energetic off-nominal signature — a visible flash, fire, or explosive event at or within seconds of a start command; a hard-start or combustion-instability acknowledgment; ignition-transient damage to an engine or bay; or an anomaly propagating to adjacent engines during a multi-engine start — as distinct from, or in addition to, mere non-start or lag. Graded on webcast and SpaceX statements.

▶ CALL A5 — p ≥ 0.78 — ESCALATION BEYOND THE INITIATING ENGINE (grades only if an energetic transient under Call A4 occurs): the event does not remain contained to the initiating engine — adjacent-engine effects, bay or feedline damage, or a vehicle-level consequence (attitude excursion, early burn termination, loss of stage) follows in the same phase. Graded on webcast, SpaceX statements, independent tracking. Pre-committed: if no energetic transient under Call A4 occurs, this call is VOID — never counted as a hit or a miss.

RECOMMENDATION (unchanged, reinforced): scrub — with review of recent static-fire and engine-test start-transient data for signatures of elevated energy release or instability, and evaluation of detection-and-response timelines for an energetic event versus a simple non-ignition, particularly in the multi-engine booster start and at the critical relights.

Terms: opinion from a disclosed methodology; no non-public information; no assertion about any company's internal knowledge or condition; never revised after seal. The SHA-256 of this addendum enters the record as a companion seal to LA-023 — the original 17 July seal and its twelve calls are unchanged and unchangeable.

ADDENDUM 3 TO LA-023 — SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · INCOMPLETE PURIFICATION HYPOTHESIS

Addendum 1 committed to how foreign material may have entered the system; Addendum 2 to how an altered ignition can amplify. This addendum commits to why the system may not have returned to a known-good state despite the time, conditioning, and hardware intervention already applied.

THE HYPOTHESIS: a portion of any foreign material, reaction byproducts, or moisture within the propellant-wetted surfaces, injector elements, or internal passages remains suspended or loosely adhered rather than being driven off or captured by the normal processes that should restore cleanliness — thermal cycling, propellant flushing, filtration, drains, and static firing. The system therefore carries a persistently unsettled internal state into flight: fine particulates or reaction products present in the propellant and combustion environment that were not present in prior configurations. Expected expressions: extended time to reach stable temperatures, pressures, or flow during chill-down and conditioning; intermittent, variable restrictions as suspended material lodges and releases in orifices, screens, and valve seats; material driven into critical ignition zones precisely at start commands; and re-mobilization of settled material under the changed acceleration, vibration, and thermal environment after approximately T+6 — with the low-acceleration coast before the vacuum relight and the first operational Starlink V3 deployment as the phase where any remaining suspension is least recoverable. Under this hypothesis, hardware swaps and additional conditioning may continue to show improvement without reaching the stable internal state demonstrated on previous successful configurations.

This is a hypothesis derived from the record's disclosed, unconventional timing methodology and from public information only. It asserts no engineering fact, imputes no negligence, no process failure, and no knowledge to SpaceX or any person; SpaceX's own inspection and test data may already exclude it — the public record before the window does not show that. It addresses the mission and the day, never any security.

One graded call, sealed under the same discipline — public evidence only, misses kept at full weight:

▶ CALL A6 — p ≥ 0.78 — PERSISTENCE SIGNATURES BEFORE FLIGHT (from this seal through liftoff): the path to flight itself shows the unsettled-state signature — at least one of: an additional static fire or engine-test campaign before the retry; a further window slip publicly attributed to vehicle or propulsion checkouts rather than weather or range; or a countdown hold, recycle, or abort tied to engine conditioning, chill-down, or propellant state. Graded on SpaceX's public schedule and webcast callouts.

RECOMMENDATION (unchanged, reinforced): scrub — with analysis of recent static-fire and test data for delayed-stabilization signatures, assessment of whether any change in feedstock, conditioning procedures, or ground-support equipment could have introduced material that resists normal separation, and verification that filtration and conditioning are removing, rather than redistributing, any suspended material — before the first operational-payload flight is committed.

ADDENDUM 4 TO LA-023 — SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · DIMINISHING RETURNS ON A NEW CONFIGURATION

Addenda 1–3 committed to what may be wrong and how it may manifest. This addendum commits to the decision logic above them: whether the current state of this new configuration justifies flight at all.

THE HYPOTHESIS: Booster 20 and Ship 40 are a meaningfully new configuration — V3 architecture, updated Raptor hardware, revised start sequences, and first-time objectives — flying only the second flight of the design, now carrying its first operational cargo. The 16 July abort and the two-engine replacement were the configuration's first corrective cycle. The reading is that the system now sits in a partially stabilized but still non-nominal condition: additional conditioning is producing real improvement, but with diminishing returns — each further cycle yields smaller gains while carrying its own risk of introducing new variables or stressing already-exercised components — and the configuration has not yet demonstrated the homogeneous, repeatable behavior of prior successful vehicles. The risk posture is therefore asymmetric: the downside of flying a still-maturing configuration with operational Starlink V3 satellites and a first in-space relight objective is materially larger than the downside of taking additional time — and pushing for one more increment of improvement on the pad risks regression rather than convergence, particularly once the vehicle enters the high-intensity regime after T+0 and the shifted dynamics after approximately T+6.

This is a characterization derived from the record's disclosed, unconventional timing methodology and from public information only — the flight history, the 16 July abort, and the publicly announced remedies. It is not an engineering audit; it asserts no fact about the vehicle's internal state, imputes no negligence and no knowledge to SpaceX or any person, and SpaceX's own readiness criteria may already establish everything this addendum questions. It addresses the mission and the day, never any security.

One graded call, sealed under the same discipline — public evidence only, misses kept at full weight:

▶ CALL A7 — p ≥ 0.78 — REGRESSION UNDER FURTHER INTERVENTION (event-anchored: grades only if SpaceX performs additional corrective or conditioning work beyond the two-engine replacement before liftoff — a further engine change, an additional static fire or spin prime, or repeated propellant-load cycles): that further work is followed, before or during the next launch attempt, by a NEW publicly visible vehicle issue or a further vehicle-attributed slip — the diminishing-returns signature of a system being pushed past the flat of its improvement curve — rather than a clean single pass to flight. Graded on SpaceX's public schedule, statements, and webcast. Pre-committed: if no further intervention beyond the swap occurs, this call is VOID — never counted as a hit or a miss.

The rest of this addendum's thesis is deliberately expressed through calls already sealed: the persistence signatures of Call A6, and the first-time-objective calls 10, 11, and 12 — a still-maturing configuration reveals itself exactly there. No additional bins are claimed for it.

RECOMMENDATION (unchanged, completed by this lens): scrub — and rather than continuing incremental cycles without a defined endpoint, define objective homogeneity criteria for this configuration first: what clean, repeatable behavior across T-0 ignition, ascent, staging, booster relights, coast, and vacuum relight must look like before a flight carrying operational cargo and first-time objectives is committed. The elevated consequence of this specific mission justifies a higher readiness threshold than a pure test article would require.

companions.claim:
ADDENDUM 5 TO LA-023 — SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · PROGRESSIVE SOLID-FRAGMENTATION HYPOTHESIS · THE FINAL ADDENDUM

Addenda 1–4 committed to the origin (common-mode contamination), the ignition character (energetic transients), the persistence (incomplete purification), and the decision logic (diminishing returns on a new configuration). This fifth and final addendum commits to how the condition may express structurally — on the vehicle and on the payload stack itself. The reading is closed with this seal; nothing further will be added before the window.

THE HYPOTHESIS: progressive structural fragmentation driven by repeated discrete pressure transients acting in combination with multi-axis vibration and thermal gradients. Solid components detach in successive events rather than a single impulsive overload: a pressure transient — tank ullage, engine-bay, or purge-line — exceeds local structural margin at a tile attachment, weld, or stringer interface and liberates a first fragment; the resulting surface discontinuity or minor leak amplifies subsequent pressure spikes; each new transient frees additional material — heat-shield tiles, stainless-steel skin panels, flap segments, aft-structure pieces — at intervals of seconds to tens of seconds. Cumulative mass loss and surface disruption degrade thermal protection and control authority; the terminal outcomes are a controlled abort or breakup with an expanding debris field. Primary vulnerable zones: the windward heat-shield tile field, aft skirt and engine-bay interfaces, and forward flap hinges. The REPEATED pressure-correlated signature — successive ejections, each coinciding with a pressure spike, vibration rise, or localized temperature excursion — distinguishes this mode from the isolated, intermittent tile loss seen on prior flights. The same cyclic physics applies to the Starlink V3 stack and its deployment hardware: successive pressure-driven ejections can originate from satellite structural panels, solar-array substrates, deployment latches and hinges, or stack-to-ship interfaces — each loss changing stack mass properties and separation dynamics, reducing functional satellite count or insertion accuracy. Real-time precursors, stated in advance so the mode is observable from public coverage: recurring pressure transients beyond nominal envelopes; rising broadband vibration or new high-frequency content phase-locked to those transients; localized surface-temperature rise immediately following a pressure event. Two or more concurrent indicators — especially recurring pressure spikes — constitute the cascade signature.

This is a hypothesis derived from the record's disclosed, unconventional timing methodology and from public information only — including the publicly observed history of intermittent tile loss and aft-section events on prior Starship flights. It asserts no engineering fact about the vehicle's internal state, imputes no negligence, no design or process failure, and no knowledge to SpaceX or any person; SpaceX's own structural margins and abort logic may already bound everything described here. It addresses the mission and the day, never any security.

Two graded calls, sealed under the same discipline — public evidence only, misses kept at full weight:

▶ CALL A8 — p ≥ 0.78 — PROGRESSIVE FRAGMENTATION, VEHICLE SIDE (either stage, any phase from liftoff through splashdown): repeated solid-fragment ejection manifests — multiple discrete ejection events (tiles, skin panels, flap segments, or aft-structure pieces) visible on the webcast or external/onboard cameras or acknowledged by SpaceX, occurring in succession within a phase or across two or more phases, or fragment release following a pressure or energetic event — as distinct from a single isolated tile loss. Candor, sealed with the call: prior Starship flights have shown intermittent tile loss on the public record, so isolated shedding alone is near the demonstrated base rate; this call stakes the REPEATED, PROGRESSIVE form — the successive, recurring pattern — and is priced by the record's engine on that sharpened claim only. Graded on webcast, camera views, and SpaceX statements.

▶ CALL A9 — p ≥ 0.78 — PAYLOAD-STACK DEGRADATION PATHWAY (grades only if the Starlink V3 payload objective falls short — original Call 10): the shortfall's public evidence is consistent with mechanical degradation of the stack or deployment hardware — partial or failed release, latch, hinge, or deployment-mechanism anomalies, damaged or non-functional satellites after release, or insertion inaccuracy observable in public tracking — rather than a purely guidance, software, or commanding cause. Pre-committed: if Call 10 does not fire, this call is VOID — never counted as a hit or a miss. Graded on SpaceX statements, webcast, and independent satellite tracking.

RECOMMENDATION (unchanged, completed by this final lens): scrub — and should the program proceed, the advisory-form focus this hypothesis makes concrete: consider progressive-failure abort logic that triggers on two or more sequential pressure-consistent fragment signatures rather than a single peak load; consider combined multi-axis vibration, thermal-cycle, and repeated pressure-pulse qualification of heat-shield attachments and the full payload-stack release hardware at margins exceeding expected flight loads, with residual attachment margins quantified after cyclic loading; consider ground-side classifiers tuned to recurring pressure transients correlated with fragment events, with continuous visual coverage of tile fields and the payload bay; and consider pre-loaded partial-payload activation and delayed-deployment sequences so surviving satellites can still achieve functional orbits if stack integrity is compromised. This mode is cumulative and cyclic — the repeated transients both drive and reveal the progressive loss, making the cascade observable and, with thresholds set in advance, potentially interruptible before terminal breakup.

Terms: opinion from a disclosed methodology; no non-public information; no assertion about any company's internal knowledge or condition; never revised after seal. With this seal, the LA-023 reading is closed.

LA-023 · SEALED ERRATUM E1 — 19 JULY 2026, BEFORE ANY RETARGETED WINDOW

One sentence of the sealed assessment of 17 July requires correction of record: "Scope: Call 1 is specific to the 20 July window; all flight calls attach to Flight 13 whenever it flies, through 3 August 2026."

The clause was introduced by the desk during drafting as schedule-robustness language. It was not the operator's instruction, and it contradicts the record's founding and long-published method law — Time Is the Address: every reading derives from, and binds to, the exact launch time it was cast for. A reading of the 20 July window cannot follow the vehicle to a different launch time. A different day is a different sky.

Correction, effective at this erratum's seal: the clause is inoperative. No call of LA-023 grades against any launch time other than the 20 July window. With SpaceX's official retarget of 19 July (22:12 UTC), that window is resolved: what resolved at the window grades now; every call conditional on the flight being flown is VOID — and the renunciation is symmetric and irrevocable: from the flight of 23 July or any later date, this record claims no hits and accepts no misses. Twenty of the twenty-two sealed calls, each carrying a committed probability, are surrendered before that window exists — the cost of the correction is the proof of its good faith.

The sealed texts of 17–18 July are unchanged and unchangeable; this erratum joins the record beside them as a sealed companion, under the same discipline: never revised, graded in public. The drafting error was the desk's; the operator caught it. A sealed record corrects itself the only honest way it can — forward, in the open, before the outcome.

MASTER FRAME — LA-023 · SEALED 18 JULY 2026, BEFORE THE WINDOW · HOW TO READ THIS ADVISORY

THE OVERARCHING CALL, stated once and sealed: Flight 13, as currently configured, carries an elevated-anomaly condition into its window. ONE OR MORE of the anomaly classes named across the sealed assessment of 17 July and its sealed addenda manifests, in some combination, within their scoped windows — committed at p ≥ 0.90.

The graded calls beneath this frame are the named expressions of that single condition — the way a forecast of one storm names the dam, the river, and the buildings. Not every expression must occur for the condition to be real: the storm that only breaks the dam was still the storm. The expressions span the entire path, not only the flight: a propulsion-cause scrub of the window, a checkout-attributed slip, or a conditioning-class countdown abort (the classes of Call 1 and Call A6) is as much an expression of this condition as anything that happens after liftoff — the storm can break before the engines ever light. Read the named calls equally as the checklist of where to look: they are the areas a readiness review would verify, stated in advance, with the desk's commitment that one or more of them will manifest. Every named call will nonetheless be graded individually, at full weight, misses included — the discipline is the product, and no combination of hits excuses any individual miss from the ledger.

How this frame is priced, sealed with it: this master call grades ONLY against the named classes — never against “anything goes wrong.” A hurricane warning is not graded on “weather happened”; it is graded on the corridors it named and the coastline it cleared, and it is validated when any named corridor floods. The same rule binds here, in both directions: a weather or range scrub, an avionics or software fault, a ground-systems failure outside the named classes — none of these validates this frame, because the frame excluded them. The record’s published engine prices the master call as the blind mass of the named bins in the frozen partitions, and it earns only that; the concentrated information — what this desk is staking — lives in WHICH named expressions manifest, in what combination, at the committed envelope, under the sealed claim that the remedies already undertaken do not clear the condition.

This document is also an example of the decision-input a principal receives from this desk before a window: its value to a reader is the phase-by-phase description available in advance — sealed, priced, and graded in public afterward, whatever happens.

Terms: opinion from a disclosed methodology; no non-public information; no assertion about any company's internal knowledge or condition; never revised after seal.
